story:
The store just opened. Monica is at the register. Rule: ask if they found everything. First customer: a man with bad breath, an outie under a black tee, and meth mouth. He pushes a dolly stacked with monster TVs and top-shelf home theater boxes—months of her pay. Others in black drift across the floor: two skanky guys, a chubby woman with haystack hair. A van backs toward the doors.

Rule: if it’s more than you make in a week, call the manager. Monica gives the coded “Price check ten.” No answer. The fisheye mirror shows customers, no manager, no customer service. She moves slow; twelve slow scans slow her more. Her thumb taps the panic button.

Rule: always be polite. “Your card, please.” He flips a scuffed card. The strip reads “Patric…ley.” Rule: repeat the customer’s name. “Patric…ley.” He stares past her at the van. Rule: swipe the card. The reader flashes “Patricia Dooley.” Monica knows Pat: a gamer, a rule-quoter, someone tough. The signature strip is rubbed to a loopy P and a “ley” no guy would write. Rule: look at the signature. Wrong. Rule: if something is wrong with a credit card, call the card company.

She calls. Stumbles on the code. Thinks: Just hit Complete, let them go. Thinks of Wally the manager and Chet from customer service—how they mocked her, threatened her job, crossed lines. Pat Dooley’s steady voice in her head: That’s harassment. Mom’s voice: Keep the job.

The card-security girl comes on. “Yes or no?” Monica answers: yes, they’re here, more than one, the card’s wrong, resend, she’s scared. She sees a knife in the guy’s pocket. “Yes.” Rule: smile. She does. “Verifying transaction,” she says aloud.

“Print. Get a signature. Let them leave. Stay on the line.” He scribbles; they roll out. The skanky guys vanish. Haystack hair stands by the open van.

Card Security: “Police are en route. If they come back, hide.”

Monica thinks about forty years at minimum wage. She sees Wally and Chet finally coming up behind her, reflected in the register’s sheen. Haystack hair steps back inside with a shotgun. Monica hits the floor. One blast. Plastic, paper, and cash rain down. A magazine bounces off her head.

“You OK?” the card-security girl asks. “Yes.” Behind the counter, groaning. Monica waits a long time. Officers arrive. Haystack hair in cuffs. A shotgun bagged. EMTs over Wally and Chet.

Card Security asks for an officer; Monica hands over the phone. She studies the scene and connects the rules. Rule: the store has been open five minutes. That much gear already on a dolly—staged. Rule: a manager is at the register within a minute. They never came. They didn’t answer her panic call. Wally and Chet were in on it. The woman returned to clean up loose ends and shot them. The cops take notes; gurneys roll.

Monica hears Pat again: You’re done here. She hears her own first Rule, sharper now than the script on the employee poster: Protect yourself. Trust what you see. Call for help. Let the bad guys leave. Live. She looks at the ruin of the register, the empty doorway, the handcuffed woman, the managers who sold her out. Minimum wage plus two bits doesn’t buy blood.

She stands, steady. The End, but not for her.
